records.ety: [OE. enspiren, OF. enspirer, inspirer, F. inspirer, fr. L. inspirare; pref. in- in + spirare to breathe. See Spirit.]

defs: To breathe into; to fill with the breath; to animate.
quotes: When Zephirus eek, with his sweete breath, Inspird hath in every holt and heath The tender crops.
Descend, ye Nine, descend and sing, The breathing instruments inspire.

defs: To infuse by breathing, or as if by breathing.
quotes: He knew not his Maker, and him that inspired into him an active soul.

defs: To draw in by the operation of breathing; to inhale; -- opposed to expire.
quotes: Forced to inspire and expire the air with difficulty.

defs: To infuse into the mind; to communicate to the spirit; to convey, as by a divine or supernatural influence; to disclose preternaturally; to produce in, as by inspiration.
quotes: And generous stout courage did inspire.
But dawning day new comfort hath inspired.

defs: To infuse into; to affect, as with a superior or supernatural influence; to fill with what animates, enlivens, or exalts; to communicate inspiration to; as, to inspire a child with sentiments of virtue; to inspire a person to do extraordinary feats.
quotes: Erato, thy poet's mind inspire, And fill his soul with thy celestial fire.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Inspired; p. pr. & vb. n. Inspiring.]

defs: To draw in breath; to inhale air into the lungs; -- opposed to expire.

quotes: And when the wind amongst them did inspire, They wavd like a penon wide dispread.
